tipRules.tip: Hackney: 'A great open water lure for fall. You can cover water, fish grass, and fish flats with 2-8 feet of water. Fish are more aggressive due to weather and daylight change.'
Hackney: 'Use the Tungsten 2 Tap version for pressured fish - the single tungsten rattle creates a subtle tap-tap-tap sound totally unique from the standard rattling version.'
Hackney: 'Use the silent version for post-frontal conditions, clear water, bright blue skies, or minimal wind - conditions that put bass in a negative mood.'
Hackney: 'Use the 1/4-ounce version early in fall when bass are feeding on smaller bait and reluctant to eat larger offerings.'
Hackney: 'The 1/2-ounce version is my mainstay. Use the 3/4-ounce for super aggressive fish or trophy fisheries like Amistad or Falcon.'
Hackney: 'In clear water they often hit in the first 3-4 cranks. If you catch 2-3 in a row on the first 10 feet and then the bite stops, change up the retrieve all the way back to the boat.'
Hackney: 'In warm water around grass, rip the lipless crankbait. In colder water, pull it, almost worming it through the grass because they are not that aggressive.'

Hackney: 'When bass are spot-oriented inside a pocket of grass or on a depression in a rock pile, pitch the lipless crank and let it fall right into the sweet spot. It shimmies as it falls like a dying baitfish.'

knowledge.insight: Hackney: 'Big fish relate to the perimeter, a creek channel, and hold on an isolated piece of grass on a flat. Little fish run up shallow while big fish satellite around the area.'

Hackney: 'During winter drawdown, larger fish will be on the flat out front holding on a stump, even in 2-3 feet of water, always relating to solid structure like a drop-off, rock pile, piece of wood, or isolated grass.'
Hackney: 'Always upsize hooks with short-shank trebles at least one size bigger than factory. 1/4-ounce gets a No.4, 1/2-ounce gets a No.2, 3/4-ounce gets a No.1.'
Hackney: 'Line test dictates running depth. Lighter line runs deeper. 20-pound fluorocarbon is the mainstay. Use a 7-4 medium-heavy composite rod for flats and a 7-foot rod for the 1/4-ounce model.'
